title: Loyal but Powerless: The Downgrading of Russia’s Elite
gist: Based on years of interviews with Russian technocrats, the paper argues Russia's ruling stratum is not an elite with political agency but a community of ~200-300 'nobiles' systematically stripped of the capacity to change the system through a quarter-century of deliberate depoliticization. The downstream cost is that post-Putin Russia will face a critical deficit of agency, making democratization unlikely and elite conflict over a collapsing system the more probable outcome.

themes.theme: Russia's elite has no political agency

insights.claim: Russia's ruling stratum should be understood not as an 'elite' in the Mosca/Pareto sense but as Roman-style 'nobiles' — roughly 200-300 senior officials, security chiefs, and state/private company CEOs who have access to Putin but no influence over strategic decisions.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Investors should not expect Russian technocrats or oligarchs to act as an internal brake on Kremlin decisions; there is no constituency capable of moderating geopolitical risk from within.
insights.quote: Russia’s nobiles had access to Putin and his inner circle—but access is not the same as influence.

insights.claim: In 2022 Russia's nobiles possessed concrete knowledge that war was economically irrational — hundreds of billions in seizable Western-held reserves, import dependence, and a budget assuming global integration — but had lost even the capacity to articulate an alternative.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Economic rationality is no longer a predictor of Kremlin behavior; sanctions and reserve seizures do not produce internal policy correction.
insights.quote: They had lost not only any influence over the decision, but the capacity to articulate an alternative.

insights.claim: The nobiles retain the 'agency to adapt' (survive, optimize, crisis-manage) but are structurally deprived of the 'agency to change' the system's goals or offer alternatives, explaining both their effective handling of the 2022 economic shock and their political passivity.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Bet on Russian institutional resilience to shocks, but not on institutional course-correction; the system absorbs crises rather than reversing decisions.
insights.locator: The war in Ukraine has laid bare

insights.claim: Depoliticization of Russia's elite was not spontaneous but an engineered institutional environment built through normalized Putin precedents, and is likely to persist after Putin leaves because it is embedded in institutions, not his personality.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: A leadership transition will not reset Russia's governance logic; the environment outlasts the leader, so post-Putin political risk may not decline.
insights.quote: It is this environment, and not the personality of the leader, that is the subject of analysis here, because it is very likely that this environment will remain in place even after Putin is gone.

insights.claim: The 2003 arrest of Yukos owner Mikhail Khodorkovsky, followed by 2004-2005 law-enforcement raids on Vekselberg (Renova), Fridman (TNK, Alfa), Bendukidze, Gutseriev (Slavneft), and Kiselyov (Renaissance Capital), established that political activity was incompatible with retaining private control of major assets.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Russian asset ownership is conditional on political loyalty, not legal title — a structural risk premium for any exposure to Russian private capital.
insights.quote: Putin was demonstrating that the main value of any entrepreneur was not their success in business, but their political and personal loyalty.
insights.locator: But even then, many business leaders

insights.claim: The 2007 Cherkesov episode taught three durable rules: disputes must be resolved behind the scenes, loyalty matters more than merit or long personal ties to Putin, and no part of the security apparatus may become an independent power center.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Public signs of elite infighting are deliberately suppressed, so visible elite conflict is a rare and meaningful signal when it does surface.
insights.quote: Loyalty was more important than merit.
insights.locator: Cherkesov served as a cautionary tale

insights.claim: By late 2014 Putin openly institutionalized 'manual control' of the economy as standard procedure, turning the bottom-up government approval system into a ritual that formalizes pre-determined results.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Formal Russian institutional process carries little information; real decisions are made informally before meetings, so monitoring official channels misleads.
insights.quote: The bottom-up approval system became no more than a ritual devoid of substance.

insights.claim: Using Howard Becker's 'moral career' concept, the paper argues Russian technocrats gradually internalize role norms until they stop acknowledging any gap between group morality and external morality — making their transformation, unlike Becker's deviant cases, far less reversible.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Do not expect a wave of conscience-driven defections from inside the Russian state; the conditioning is deep and self-reinforcing.
insights.quote: a Russian technocrat, unlike a jazz musician or marijuana user, over time stops acknowledging the gulf between the group norms and external morals.

insights.claim: Russian governance runs on closely guarded informal practices — presidential resolutions emailed to favored businessmen bypassing administrative procedure, verbal Putin orders to support specific state capitalists, and trillion-ruble issues settled without formal meetings.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Access to informal decision channels, not formal position, determines who benefits from state resource allocation in Russia.
insights.quote: Issues that have trillions of rubles at stake are agreed upon without so much as a formal meeting.
insights.locator: These practices come in various forms

insights.claim: From the mid-2010s, accelerating after the 2014 Crimea annexation, FSB officers embedded in ministries and state companies monitored employees' 'moral character' — vacations, associates, attitude to Navalny and protests — with reports kept by the FSB, and by 2022 this was common knowledge.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Surveillance of the state workforce itself reinforces silence; the cost of dissent is internalized well before any open challenge could form.

insights.claim: Stress tests run a month before the 2022 invasion projected a severe scenario: GDP contraction over 10 percent, unemployment rising from 4 to 12 percent, banks losing 5-6 trillion rubles, rates at 20-30 percent, and the ruble at 150-200 to the dollar.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: The Kremlin's technocrats knew the downside in advance and executed crisis playbooks anyway — quantifies the institutional reflexes that blunted sanctions impact.
insights.quote: the ruble would plummet to 150–200 against the dollar.
insights.locator: In the first few hours of the invasion

insights.claim: Russia built 'Fortress Russia' in 2014-2015 — corporate de-dollarization, new bank FX rules, designating state banks 'too big to fail,' and de-risking the balance of payments — measures that made market sense but also hardened the regime against external pressure.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Russia's sanctions resilience was pre-built years before 2022; future sanctions face an economy deliberately structured to absorb them.

insights.claim: Individual sanctions 'cemented the elites' rather than splitting them: sanctioned businesspeople complained of unfairness but not about the Kremlin, concluding they will be Russia-based for the next 10-15 years and will make their lives there.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Personal sanctions on Russian elites entrench loyalty rather than fostering defection — a counterintuitive effect for those expecting sanctions to fracture the regime.
insights.locator: The reaction of the nobiles to personal sanctions

insights.claim: Since the full-scale war Russia has stopped disclosing a significant share of its economic and legal statistics, leaving even senior officials and businesspeople working with incomplete or skewed pictures of reality.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Analysts cannot rely on Russian official data; opacity is now structural and affects insiders too, raising the error bars on any Russia macro call.
insights.quote: This information is not just unavailable to external observers, but to officials and businesspeople too.

insights.claim: 1990s-era Russian businessmen and officials, with a deal-focused black-and-white worldview, see Donald Trump as 'one of them' — a self-made man rather than old money — and project their hopes for sanctions relief onto him.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Russian elite expectations of sanctions easing are pinned to Trump personally; any U.S. policy shift would be read through that lens by Russian capital.
insights.quote: They see him as one of them: a self-made man, as opposed to coming from old money.
insights.locator: This logic is especially close to the hearts

insights.claim: Capital has 'returned home' to Russia but, lacking legal protection, business is being forced back into local and regional politics — cultivating governors, local FSB, and prosecutors for basic protection — reproducing the system's personalistic logic rather than challenging it.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Watch for a quiet decentralization of Russian power toward regional configurations as Putin focuses on geopolitics — relevant for understanding where real protection and leverage will sit.
insights.quote: business interests find themselves forced to return to politics as a way of ensuring predictability, protection, and stability.
insights.locator: The irony is that the Kremlin

insights.claim: Once Putin leaves, the depoliticized elite's lack of experience in strategic thinking and working with alternatives means the vacuum will most likely be filled by inter-elite conflict over a collapsing system rather than by reformers, making democratization minimal.
insights.speaker: Alexandra Prokopenko
insights.so_what: Position for a chaotic, contested Russian transition rather than a smooth liberal opening; post-Putin Russia is unlikely to automatically become a different Russia.
insights.quote: The void created by a depoliticized ruling elite won’t necessarily be filled by democratic actors. It will be filled by whatever force proves to be best organized at the moment a political vacuum emerges.
insights.locator: For Western observers, there can be one conclusion
